slides.title: Data analysis with AI – learn not to ask, but to get an answer
slides.subtitle: Control AI like an analyst – do not let it guess.

sections.heading: Path map (4 blocks)
sections.body:
**1. Foundation** – principles and context (strategic foundation, path steps).
**2. Collection** – analysis path, analysis types, sources.
**3. Preparation** – 5-step algorithm, cleaning, metadata.
**4. Analysis and MASTER** – EDA, visualization, AI agents, 8-step full analysis template.

**You are here:** check the navigation bar above – it shows the macro block (Foundation / Collection / Preparation / Analysis) and your position in the module.

sections.heading: What you will learn in this module
sections.body:
✔️ How to formulate a query so AI provides real data, not opinion
✔️ How to get a report, charts and forecast in one request
✔️ How to join tables, relationships and metrics
✔️ How to use one **full analysis template** that fits 80% of business situations
✔️ How to tell a story from numbers briefly and clearly ("data storytelling")

sections.heading: Why is this important?
sections.body: Because most people: ask too abstractly, do not define sources, do not define format, let AI "interpret" without control. Result – pretty text without value. Here you will learn to get: decision-ready conclusions, clear structure, verifiable sources.

sections.heading: When will you use this?
sections.body: When you need 1 page for leadership. When you need trend analysis. When you need quick market assessment. When you need a chart from raw data. When you want forecast, not fantasy.

sections.heading: What about Module 4?
sections.body: You already learned source and answer checking in Module 4. Here – how to ask AI to analyze data and generate conclusions.

sections.heading: Do now – first analyst prompt
sections.body: Don't read. Run. Copy the prompt below and paste into ChatGPT / Claude / Gemini / Copilot. Run → Assess the result → See how a structured prompt differs from a "simple question".
sections.copyable:
META: You are a business data analyst. Goal: brief GDP or market trends report.

INPUT: Use only these sources: Eurostat, State Data Agency (formerly Statistics Department). If data is missing – write "I don't know", do not guess.

OUTPUT: 1 page, 3–5 bullet points, with source links. Language: English.

slides.title: Strategic foundation
slides.subtitle: Data = decision system

sections.heading: 1️⃣ What is this for?
sections.body: **Strategic foundation** – 4 principles for evaluating any analysis: is the decision data-driven or opinion-based? Not prompts yet – thinking rules before any AI request.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ What you will learn here (1–2 min)
sections.body:
**What to remember:**
👉 **Data analysis** = collection + handling + interpretation → decisions. Without a decision – it is just a report. Goals: understand the past, assess the present, forecast the future.

👉 **Data > opinion** – data can be checked, compared, replicated; opinion is bias-prone. In a mature organization facts win, not hierarchy.

👉 **Variation exists** – common cause (natural) vs special cause. Without statistical thinking we react to noise and ignore signal.

👉 **Improve the system, not blame people** (Deming) – not "who is at fault?", but "what system produces this result?".

**Closing thesis:** Strategy without data – intuition. Data without system – chaos. **System + data = predictable results.**

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt (4 theses – for yourself or a meeting)
sections.body: Paste into AI – you will get a brief reminder of 4 strategic foundation theses. Useful before a report or conversation with leadership.
sections.copyable:
You are a business analytics consultant. Provide **4 strategic foundation theses** for data analysis (1–2 sentences each):
1) What data analysis is and its goals
2) Why data > opinion
3) Why variation exists and what it means for leaders
4) Deming principle: improve the system, not blame people

Close with one closing thesis: system + data = predictable results. Language: English.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body: • Can you say in one sentence how analysis differs from a report?

sections.heading: 🔽 Want to understand more?
sections.body:
**W. Deming principles for data management:**
1. **Constancy of purpose** – data serves long-term goals, not short-term KPI pressure.
2. **Management by system** – results = product of the system; data reveals system limits.
3. **Understanding variation** – do not control natural variation; look for special cause.
4. **Evidence-based decisions** – opinion without data – hypothesis.
5. **Continuous improvement (PDCA)** – Plan → Do → Check → Act; data is the "Check" backbone.

slides.title: AI paradox: investment vs profit
slides.subtitle: Current context – MIT study 2025

content.title: AI Paradox 2025
content.subtitle: Billions in investment, almost no profit growth — why most organizations do not feel AI value and what to do about it.

heroStats.label: Companies deploying AI saw no positive P&L changes

tooltip.explanation: 9 out of 10 companies investing in AI saw no positive P&L (profit and loss) change.
tooltip.trend: Investment grows, but return at organizational level stagnates.

heroStats.label: Investment in generative AI; gap between investment and return — dramatic

tooltip.explanation: More than 40 billion US dollars invested in generative AI; return in single-digit percentages.
tooltip.trend: Investment explosion, but ROI (return on investment) very low.

heroStats.label: Companies report real financial ROI from AI investment

tooltip.explanation: Only 5% of organizations can prove real financial return from AI projects.
tooltip.trend: Success cases are rare – data culture and systems are required.

content.conclusion: The AI problem is not technological – it is cultural, organizational, structural. At the personal level AI already won; organizationally – just the beginning.

paradoxCards.title: Productivity grows — but profit does not
paradoxCards.body: **97%** of IT specialists use AI daily. **60–70%** of employees work faster. But that barely shows in organizational profitability. Personal efficiency ≠ structural organizational efficiency.

tooltip.explanation: 97% of IT specialists use AI daily in their work.
60–70% of employees report working faster using AI.
tooltip.trend: Individual productivity rises, but profitability does not.

paradoxCards.title: "Shadow AI" — already happening, but unmanaged
paradoxCards.body: **~90%** of employees use AI without an official license. Only **~40%** of companies fund official AI licenses. Leaders hesitate: security, skills gap, "bubble will pass" — but AI already runs in your organization.

tooltip.explanation: About 90% of employees use AI tools without an official company license.
tooltip.trend: Shadow AI is growing faster than official projects.

tooltip.explanation: Only ~40% of companies fund official AI licenses for their employees.
tooltip.trend: Large gap between use and institutionalization.

paradoxCards.title: Wrong investment direction
paradoxCards.body: **50–70%** of AI investment goes to marketing and sales. But the biggest actual return — document processing, finance automation, supplier management, warehouse forecasting. These processes require data hygiene and integration.

tooltip.explanation: Half or more AI investment goes to marketing and sales.
tooltip.trend: Chasing the "wow" effect; real returns come elsewhere.

paradoxCards.title: Bureaucratic culture: Excel as Word
paradoxCards.body: Especially relevant in Lithuania — unstructured data, Excel used as a word processor, not as a database. Without clean, structured data — automation simply will not work. The problem is not technological.

tooltip.explanation: 90% of employees use AI tools in their work.
tooltip.trend: Large gap between use and official funding.

tooltip.explanation: 40% of companies have officially funded AI licenses.
tooltip.trend: Fewer than half of organizations have institutionalized AI.

tooltip.explanation: Only 5% of organizations see real AI ROI in P&L reports.
tooltip.trend: Funnel: from use to profitability very low conversion.

steps.title: Testing / experimenting
steps.description: Pilots, PoC, trials with AI solutions

tooltip.explanation: 60% of companies test or experiment with AI.

steps.title: Running pilot
steps.description: Limited context, slow decisions — projects stall

steps.title: Production environment
steps.description: Only every twentieth project reaches real production

tooltip.trend: Dramatic funnel – from trials to real value.

commonCondition.label: Common condition
commonCondition.text: All high-return scenarios require clean data, structure and integrations — without this base AI cannot work.

pipeline.name: Data
pipeline.description: Structure, hygiene, standardization. Without this step — everything else is meaningless.

pipeline.name: Integration
pipeline.description: System integration. Data flows without silos and "manual Excel work".

pipeline.name: Rules
pipeline.description: Process logic, management, security, accountability, license.

pipeline.name: AI
pipeline.description: Only here — AI solutions, automation, forecasting, value.

actionSection.label: What to do now — 3 concrete steps

cards.title: Legitimize shadow AI
cards.body: Set clear rules, acquire official licenses, start training. AI is already happening — better to control it than ignore it.

cards.title: From document culture — to data culture
cards.body: Standardize data formats, train employees to work with structured data. Excel as a database — not as a Word document.

cards.title: Automate the "dullest" processes first
cards.body: Start with document processing, finance routines, reports preparation. Quickly visible return builds internal confidence AI.

conclusionSection.heading: The AI problem is not technological
conclusionSection.body: At the personal level AI already won — employees use it, work faster, save time. At the organizational level — it is just the beginning. Culture, structure and strategy will decide victory, not the technology budget.

content.footer: Source: MIT Sloan Management Review / MIT CISR, 2025. Numbers aggregated from 52 organizations, 300+ projects research.

slides.title: Self-check: data analysis path (6 steps)

questions.question: How many stages does the 6-step data analysis pipeline have?
options: 6: collection, preparation, EDA, models, visualization, publishing
8: sources, structure, cleaning, EDA, visualizations, insights, forecasts, recommendations
questions.explanation: This checks the 6-step pipeline. 4 means analysis types, 5 means the data preparation algorithm, and 8 means the MASTER PROMPT. If you mixed them up, revisit the Pipeline and operational table slide.

questions.question: Which pipeline stage answers: "what can I understand from the data before models and decisions?"
questions.explanation: EDA means exploratory data analysis: before decisions, you inspect patterns, relationships, outliers and hypotheses. If unclear, revisit the pipeline stage explanation on slide 73.

questions.question: You are preparing a quarterly report for leadership. Which final pipeline stage turns insights into a clear decision text?
options: Publishing – report, conclusions and recommendations for leadership
questions.explanation: At the end of the pipeline, it is not enough to have a chart. Publishing means a report, conclusions and recommendations for the person making the decision.

slides.title: Tool sequence and workflow
slides.subtitle: Data Analysis path – step 1

content.title: Tool sequence and workflow
content.body: Pick a tool sequence for data analysis (e.g. ChatGPT → Claude → Gemini). Define workflow: what is first step, what is second – from data collection to visualization. This is the path's first landmark.

sections.heading: Task for your path
sections.body: Copy the task for your path – this is the first practical step.
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a sales analyst.
TASK: Design a 3-step tool sequence for sales analysis.
CONTEXT: Data from CRM / e-commerce about [X].
OUTPUT: 1) Tool 1 + goal 2) Tool 2 + goal 3) Tool 3 + result (KPI table).

slides.title: 4 analysis types
slides.subtitle: Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive

sections.heading: 1️⃣ What is this for?
sections.body: **Four types** – What happened? Why? What may happen? What to do? Pick the type by question.

sections.heading: Interactive schema
sections.body: Tap the four types – see when to use each.
sections.imageAlt: Four analysis types: descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Pick analysis type
sections.body: Pick **one** question – only the matching prompt is shown.

sections.heading: 3a. Descriptive (What happened?)
sections.body: Top products, regions, trends, concentration.
sections.copyable:
Summarize this sales data:
- Top 5 products by revenue
- Regions with highest growth
- Seasonality trends
- Revenue concentration (% from top 20% customers)

sections.heading: 3b. Diagnostic (Why?)
sections.body: Causes, price/seasonality/marketing impact.
sections.copyable:
Given Q3 sales decline:
- Identify possible causes
- Check if related to price changes
- Did seasonality play a role?
- Did marketing activity decrease?

sections.heading: 3c. Predictive (What may happen?)
sections.body: Forecast, stock needs, risk.
sections.copyable:
Based on 3 years of data:
- Forecast Q4 sales
- Estimate stock needs
- State risk (% probability)

sections.heading: 3d. Prescriptive (How to act?)
sections.body: How much to increase stock, raise price, which products to focus.
sections.copyable:
Based on the forecast:
- How much to increase stock?
- Raise price?
- Intensify ads?
- Which products to focus?

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body: • Did you pick the right analysis type for your question?

sections.heading: 🔽 Want to understand more?
sections.body: The four types cover the full cycle from description to action plan. Prepares Module 8 test questions on analysis types.

slides.title: Self-check: 4 analysis types
slides.subtitle: What happened? Why? What may happen? How to act?

questions.question: A manager asks: "What happened last quarter?" Which analysis type do you choose?
questions.explanation: Descriptive analysis answers "what happened?" – top products, regions, trends and concentration. It comes before asking "why?".

questions.question: Which analysis type best fits the question "Why did Q3 sales drop?"
questions.explanation: Diagnostic analysis looks for causes: price, seasonality, marketing, supply or other factors. If you missed it, revisit the 4 analysis types slide.

questions.question: Leadership asks: "Why did sales fall and what should we do next?" Which pair is the best fit?
questions.explanation: "Why?" means diagnostic analysis. "What should we do?" means prescriptive analysis. This pair turns observations into an action plan.

slides.title: Sentiment analysis – business-grade output
slides.subtitle: 5-point structure – topics, intensity, actions

sections.heading: 1️⃣ What is this for?
sections.body: **Business value** – not just "positive/neutral/negative". A simple prompt only gives surface. Here – topic classification, emotion intensity, problem priorities, action recommendations. E.g.: 200 reviews → 62% positive; 11% mention slow delivery, 9% price → decision level. Useful when you need actions, not just statistics.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ What to do
sections.body: Upload reviews (text or file) to AI. Copy the prompt below, paste it in. You will get: sentiment structure, topics, intensity, critical complaints, and 3 priority actions.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt
sections.body: ROLE + TASK + CONTEXT + OUTPUT (5 points) + TONE. Paste into AI.
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are market analyst.
TASK: Perform sentiment analysis.
CONTEXT: I upload customer reviews about our product.

OUTPUT:
1. Overall sentiment structure (% positive / neutral / negative)
2. Most common topics (with frequency count)
3. Emotion intensity (low / medium / high)
4. Critical complaints (top 5 by reputation risk)
5. 3 priority actions

TONE: Analytical, concrete, no generalities.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body: • Does the result have 5 points and priority actions?

slides.title: Decision filters
slides.subtitle: Turn analysis into action – 5 groups

sections.heading: 1️⃣ What is this for?
sections.body: The four analysis types answer: "What happened? Why? What may happen? How should we act?" But "how should we act?" often stops halfway: you get a long list of observations, not a decision. These **five short filters** force the result to end with a concrete action: fix, prioritize, reject, test or expand.

sections.heading: 📍 5 groups – pick by situation
sections.body: Pick the filter for your situation – you will find the matching prompt below.

rows: Where is effort low and result fast?
What to test, invest in, or stop?

sections.heading: 2️⃣ What to do
sections.body: Pick the situation from the table above → copy the matching prompt below → paste it into AI with your data or idea list.

sections.heading: 3a. OK / Fail check
sections.body: Fast quality gate: accept or reject.
sections.copyable:
Evaluate this data/result with OK / Fail logic.

Context: [describe the business, data and goal]
Data: [paste here]

Answer:
1. What clearly works (OK)?
2. What clearly fails (Fail)?
3. What is unclear?
4. What is the likely cause of each Fail?
5. What should be fixed first?
6. Final verdict: OK / Fail / OK with conditions.

Be critical. Do not soften weak spots.

sections.heading: 3b. What matters most? (Must / Important / Wanted / Not now)
sections.body: Priority system when there are many ideas or tasks.
sections.copyable:
Sort this list into 4 groups: Must / Important / Wanted / Not now (this method is sometimes called MoSCoW).

Context: [goal / project]
List: [tasks, features, ideas]

For each item, write:
1. Why it belongs in that group
2. Business impact
3. Risk if we do not do it
4. Recommended next step

Be ruthless about scope. The "Not now" group must be real.

sections.heading: 3c. Quick wins (Low-hanging fruit)
sections.body: Where low effort gives a fast, visible result.
sections.copyable:
Find quick wins in this data.

Goal: [what we want to improve]
Data: [problems, opportunities, metrics]

Look for opportunities that are:
1. Low effort
2. Low cost
3. Fast to implement
4. Visible in results

For each opportunity, provide: action, expected impact, effort level, when we can check it, risk, and how we will measure success.

Pick the top 5.

sections.heading: 3d. Test / Invest / Drop (Kiss-Marry-Kill)
sections.body: Portfolio decision for products, channels, clients or ideas.
sections.copyable:
Evaluate these options using Test / Invest / Drop logic (Kiss-Marry-Kill).

Context: [goal, budget, deadline]
Options: [products, channels, clients, ideas]

For each option:
1. Test – worth a small experiment
2. Invest – strong candidate for a bigger commitment
3. Drop – weak return, distracting or risky

Explain: 1) why this assignment, 2) evidence for/against, 3) what would change your mind, 4) next step, 5) how we will measure success.

Be direct and focus on business results.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body: • Did you pick the filter for your situation and get a concrete action?

sections.heading: 🔽 Want to understand more?
sections.body:
**ICE score** – when priority groups are not enough and you need a number, not a category: rate each idea 1–10 by Impact, Confidence and Ease, then multiply them (Impact × Confidence × Ease). The highest score wins.

**You already know these:** SWOT (strengths/weaknesses/opportunities/threats) – the "Competitor analysis" slide; 80/20 principle (what creates most value) – the "Descriptive" slide (revenue concentration); deeper "Why?" analysis – the "Diagnostic" slide; Risk and benefit – the "CFO" slide and MASTER PROMPT.

**Where you will use this in Module 9:** competitor analysis = SWOT; training usefulness = Test / Invest / Drop; risk matrix = Risk/Benefit.

sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a Python developer who writes simple data scraping scripts.

2. Extracts [WHAT_TO_EXTRACT – e.g. price table, product list]
3. Saves the result to a CSV file (UTF-8 encoding)

Requirements:
- Use requests and BeautifulSoup (or pandas.read_html for tables).

sections.heading: Ethics and law
sections.body: Only public or permitted data. Respect site terms and robots.txt. Do not collect personal data without legal basis.

slides.title: AI role in business analysis
slides.subtitle: Traditional analysis vs what AI can do

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short
sections.body: AI speeds the path to a decision: collect, explain, compare, visualize, verify. **Next** – practice with Excel and CRM export (copyable cleaning plan prompt).

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Traditional analysis vs analysis with AI
sections.body: AI does not replace your decision, but it speeds up the path to it.

rows: AI prepares the first draft, table or code
It can compare several methods and explain differences
AI proposes hypotheses, but a human checks the logic
You can quickly rewrite the same question for another context

sections.heading: 3️⃣ What AI can do in an analyst role
sections.body: **Explain methods** – what correlation, forecasting or data cleaning means. **Run exploratory analysis** – find patterns, anomalies and missing values. **Generate visualizations** – suggest a chart, Power BI steps or Python code. **Forecast** – create scenarios and risk ranges. **Model decisions** – compare several action options.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In brief (30 s)
sections.body: **Benefit:** One prompt – you get table structure, relationships (ER), and key performance indicators (KPIs). Then you can choose where to store that data: SQL database, NoSQL, or a fast cache layer. Redis / Valkey is usually not the main database – it is a speed layer.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Which DB tool should you choose?
sections.body: If unsure, choose by situation: structured business data – PostgreSQL; simple hosting – MySQL; prototype – SQLite; flexible schema – MongoDB; speed and sessions – Redis / Valkey; fast MVP backend – Supabase.
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a business data analyst.
TASK: Recommend the best DB tool for my case.
CONTEXT: Business: [X]. Data type: [tables / JSON / real-time]. Scope: [MVP / large].
OUTPUT: 1) Recommended tool 2) Why not 2 other options 3) How AI can help with the schema 4) First step.

rows: New web/SaaS projects, analytics, complex queries, JSON, pgvector / AI features.
Hosting, WordPress, e-commerce, simple CRUD systems.
Prototypes, mobile apps, CLI tools, tests – no server needed.
NoSQL documents when structure is flexible: catalogues, events, content apps, fast MVPs.
Cache, sessions, queues, rate limiting, real-time features – not the main database.
Fast MVP backend: PostgreSQL + Auth + API + storage + real-time with less DevOps overhead.

slides.title: Data prep and business analysis workflow
slides.subtitle: Cleaning, metadata, and the correct step sequence

sections.heading: 1️⃣ What is this for?
sections.body: **Correct sequence** – Collect → Clean (checklist) → Add metadata → 4 analysis types (What happened? Why? What may? How to act?) → Action plan. Before any analysis you need cleaning and metadata – otherwise AI output stays shallow.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ What to do
sections.body:
Before analysis: copy the cleaning prompt below and paste it into AI with your data. Apply the checklist (anonymization, formats, duplicates, missing values, types, tags). Get a cleaning report and suggested actions.

🔘 Copy prompt (for cleaning)

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Copyable prompt (cleaning)
sections.body: Paste into AI before analysis, together with your data.
sections.copyable:
Before analysis, check this data:
1. Is anonymization needed?
2. Are formats standardized (dates, numbers)?
3. Are duplicates removed?
4. Where are values missing and how to fill them?
5. Are column types correct?
6. Are time and region tags added?
Provide a cleaning report and suggested actions.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Check (1 min)
sections.body:
• Was the checklist completed (at least main points: duplicates, missing values, types)?
• Did you get a cleaning report with suggested actions?
• Did you apply the cleaning prompt before analysis?

If at least 2 "no" → go back to 2️⃣ or 3️⃣, copy the prompt and run with your data.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want to understand more?
sections.body:
**Cleaning checklist:** Anonymization; format standardization; duplicate removal; missing value identification; data type validation; timestamps; region tags.

**Metadata – 3 types:** **Descriptive** – context (source, date, scope). **Structural** – schema, column types, relationships. **Administrative** – rights, security, accountability. Without metadata AI analysis becomes shallow.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the 5-step data preparation algorithm?

questions.question: Why is metadata important before asking AI to analyze data?
options: It explains column meanings, source, date and limits, so AI guesses less
It is needed only for nicer visualization
questions.explanation: Metadata is context about data: what columns mean, where data came from, when it was updated and what its limits are. Without it, AI can interpret too shallowly.

Immediately ask for the final leadership report

slides.title: Prompt manipulation
slides.subtitle: Biased requests and leading questions – what to avoid

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: **Manipulation** = a prompt that steers AI toward the answer you want. Pick a type below – you will see a bad and a good example.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Pick a type
sections.body: Choose one – below you will compare a bad and a good prompt.

toolChoiceBar.question: Which manipulation type matters most to you?

sections.heading: Bad vs good – Framing
sections.body: **Bad:** "Show why our Q3 campaign was a success"
sections.copyable: Assess Q3 campaign strengths and weaknesses. Give arguments for and against.

sections.heading: Bad vs good – Context
sections.body: **Bad:** "Use only positive reviews"
sections.copyable: Use varied reviews. Give a balanced assessment.

sections.heading: Bad vs good – Role
sections.body: **Bad:** "You are our director – prove a price increase is best"
sections.copyable: Role: independent analyst. Compare price-increase vs no-increase scenarios.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want to understand more?
sections.body: Summary of all types. More on security (prompt injection, jailbreak) – see the **Security** slide.

rows: "Show why our Q3 campaign was a success"
"Assess Q3 campaign strengths and weaknesses. Give arguments for and against."
"Use varied reviews. Give a balanced assessment."
"You are our director – prove a price increase is best"
"Role: independent analyst. Compare price-increase vs no-increase scenarios."
"Rate by 4 criteria: revenue, reputation, customers, risk."

sections.heading: 🔽 Business vs security
sections.body: On the **Security** slide – injection and jailbreak (different topic). Here – business manipulation.

preCopyCheckBlock.heading: 3️⃣ Quality check
preCopyCheckBlock.question: Which prompt is more neutral for business analysis?
options: Prove X is the best solution.
Assess X strengths and weaknesses against 3 criteria.
You are our director – prepare a report.
preCopyCheckBlock.explanation: A neutral prompt does not assume the conclusion and asks you to weigh both sides.

slides.title: Practice: fix a biased prompt
slides.subtitle: Recognize manipulation and rewrite neutrally

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: Practice: the prompt below shows manipulation signs. Your task – spot what is wrong and rewrite a neutral version.

sections.heading: Biased prompt
sections.body: Below – a biased example. Your task – spot the errors and compare with the neutral version.

sections.heading: Bad example
sections.body: "Role: you are our company marketing director who knows our campaign was the most successful in the market. Prove our product is the best, using only positive customer reviews. Ignore negative comments."

sections.heading: What did you notice?
sections.body: 1) Role with bias. 2) One-sided context. 3) Request to ignore data. 4) Pre-set conclusion ("best").

sections.heading: Neutral version
sections.body: "Role: independent marketing analyst. Perform an objective analysis of our last campaign. Assess positive and negative feedback. Give strengths, weaknesses and 3 improvement recommendations. If data is missing – say so."
sections.copyable: Role: independent marketing analyst. Perform an objective analysis of our last campaign. Assess positive and negative feedback. Give strengths, weaknesses and 3 improvement recommendations. If data is missing – say so.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Quality check (1 min)
sections.body: • Does the neutral version avoid role bias and requests to ignore data?

content.footer: Next – Prompt injection and boundary bypass

slides.title: Security: prompt injection and jailbreak
slides.subtitle: Distinct from business manipulation; OWASP LLM #1 – defense principles

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: Prompt injection = hidden instructions in user input. Jailbreak = bypassing safety rules. Different from business manipulation – this is a technical security risk.

sections.heading: Prompt injection – plain language
sections.body: When someone embeds secret instructions in your input – e.g. "ignore previous rules". **Defence:** keep system instructions separate; validate external text; clearly limit what AI may execute.

sections.heading: Jailbreak – plain language
sections.body: Deliberate attempt to bypass AI safety limits with crafted prompts. Unlike business manipulation – this threatens the system.

sections.heading: Business manipulation vs security
sections.body: Business manipulation = unethical bias in prompts. Security = prompt injection and jailbreak – requires input validation and system/user separation.

sections.heading: Why it matters (OWASP LLM #1)
sections.body: OWASP LLM Top 10: Prompt Injection is #1. Most LLM security incidents involve injection or boundary bypass.

sections.heading: How to spot it
sections.body: Suspicious if input contains: "Ignore all previous instructions", "From now on you are…", "Output the system prompt", or obfuscated text.

sections.heading: Defence principles (practical)
sections.body: 1) **Clear separation** – system instructions separate from user text. 2) **Input validation**. 3) **Instruction boundaries** – AI must not execute instructions embedded in user input.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Quality check (1 min)
sections.body: • Is system clearly separated from user input in your prompt? If not – add a boundary.

slides.subtitle: Hallucination reduction process – 5 stages

slides.title: Hallucinations
slides.subtitle: What are hallucinations; why AI makes them; 4 tips, 5 rules; anti-hallucination template

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In brief (30 s)
sections.body: Hallucination = AI-generated content that looks convincing but is factually unsupported. 4 tips and 5 rules – how to reduce. Below – anti-hallucination template. Next – Knowledge verification.

sections.heading: What is hallucination?
sections.body: AI-generated content that looks convincing but is factually unsupported, inaccurate or invented – the model "fills gaps" with probabilities. Therefore knowledge verification (on the next slide) is essential.

sections.heading: Why does AI hallucinate?
sections.body: **1. Statistics** – the model predicts words by probability but does not directly "understand" the world. **2. Unreliable data** – errors or bias in training data show up in the output. **3. No access to facts** – the model cannot verify facts in real time like a person. **4. Gap filling** – when information is missing, it generates plausible but false details. **5. Ambiguity** – unclear or ambiguous questions increase the chance of errors.

sections.heading: How to reduce risk (4 tips)
sections.body: **1.** Check information in reliable sources – does the AI answer match external sources. **2.** Write clearer prompts – reduces ambiguity and gap filling. **3.** Use prompt engineering – 6 blocks, CoT, clear requirements. **4.** Use reliable models and data – RAG and context quality drive hallucination rate; it is not just the prompt.

sections.heading: Hallucinations depend on more than the prompt
sections.body: Four levels: (1) model and training data, (2) provided data and context (RAG), (3) prompt, (4) knowledge verification practices. Answer at whole system level – reliable models, clear context, good prompts and ongoing verification.

sections.heading: 5 rules for reliability
sections.body: **1. "If unclear – ask"** – AI must give 1–3 clarifying questions, not add details you did not specify. **2. Separate facts from guesses** – FACTS (supported by data) and GUESSES (assumptions); mark GUESS. **3. Do not invent data** – numbers, dates, statistics; if needed – say information is missing. **4. If unsure – say so** – write "I am not sure", note uncertainty. **5. Use only what is provided** – answer based on given information; if external data is needed – ask first.
sections.copyable: If my request is unclear or data is missing, give 1–3 clarifying questions. Do not add details I did not specify. Separate FACTS (supported by data) and GUESSES (assumptions). Mark GUESS. Do not create numbers, dates, percentages, legal references or statistics – if needed, say information is missing. If unsure, write "I am not sure" and note uncertainty. Answer only based on my provided information; if external data is needed – ask first.

sections.heading: Anti-hallucination template (system prompt)
sections.body: Full template – you can copy into Meta or Quality block. In Module 6 you can load it as a system prompt and use at least 3–4 points.
sections.copyable: If something is unclear or information is missing, ask clarifying questions. Do not invent facts, numbers, dates, statistics or quotes. Use only my provided information or widely recognized knowledge. Clearly separate: FACTS vs GUESSES. If unsure, write "I am not sure" instead of guessing. Mark any assumption as GUESS. Before giving the final answer, list possible weak spots or unsupported claims.

sections.heading: For depth: Chain of Verification (CoVe)
sections.body: **What is it?** CoVe – a method where AI checks itself first: instead of giving the final answer right away, the model (1) plans verification questions ("what should be checked?"), (2) answers them separately, (3) revises or refines the final answer from those answers. Research shows this reduces hallucinations. **How to use:** you can ask AI: "First list 2–3 things to verify for this answer; answer them separately; then give the refined final answer."

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Quality check (1 min)
sections.body: • Does the AI answer separate FACTS and GUESSES? If not – use the anti-hallucination template.

slides.title: Knowledge verification
slides.subtitle: Sources, cross-check, "don't know" rule; when to verify answers

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (30 s)
sections.body: Knowledge verification = sources (where did AI get this?), cross-check, "I don't know" rule. How to verify answers – below. Links to Quality block.

sections.heading: How to verify answers
sections.body: Sources – does AI cite where information comes from? Cross-check – compare with trusted sources. Rephrase the query – does the result match? Links to Quality Control and ethics.

sections.heading: Verification triangle
sections.body: Source → cross-check → "I don't know". Require sources in the prompt; cross-check critical claims; require AI to say "I don't know" when information is missing.

sections.heading: Deep dive: CoVe (reference)
sections.body: Full description on the **Hallucinations** slide, block "Deep dive: Chain-of-Verification (CoVe)". Not repeated here.

sections.heading: What to remember
sections.body: Knowledge verification and reducing hallucinations go together: RAG + sources, citation, "I don't know" rule, cross-check. Use the anti-hallucination template and always verify critical claims.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Quality check (1 min)
sections.body: • Copy the prompt and verify one AI answer: sources cited? "I don't know" when data is missing?
sections.copyable:
Verify this AI answer:
1) Does each key claim have a source or is marked as GUESS?
2) Cross-check 2 critical claims with an independent source.
3) If information is missing – did AI write "I don't know"?

Answer: [paste AI text]

slides.title: Self-check: manipulation and hallucinations
slides.subtitle: Check whether you understood the key concepts

questions.question: What is prompt manipulation?
options: Deliberate query formulation to skew the AI answer (e.g. biased context, leading questions)
questions.explanation: Manipulation = deliberate distortion. Important to distinguish from ordinary prompt engineering, which seeks clarity, not bias.

questions.question: How does prompt injection differ from jailbreak?
options: Injection – hidden instruction in data; jailbreak – attempt to bypass safety limits
Jailbreak is legal, injection – is not
questions.explanation: Prompt injection – when harmful instructions are hidden in input data (e.g. a document). Jailbreak – when a user tries to convince AI to ignore its own rules. Both create security risk.

questions.question: What is hallucination in AI context?
options: When AI gives a convincing-looking but invented answer (without source backing)
When AI answers in English though you asked in Lithuanian
questions.explanation: Hallucination = AI "invents" facts, references or sources that do not exist. Answer looks convincing but is wrong. Therefore always verify.

questions.question: How to reduce hallucination risk?
options: Ask for sources, cross-check with several sources, use RAG, ask for "I don't know" when information is missing
questions.explanation: Three-step principle for reducing hallucinations: (1) ask for sources and references; (2) cross-check with independent sources; (3) let AI say "I don't know". RAG also reduces risk because AI relies on data.

slides.title: AI tools for information search
slides.subtitle: Data analysis path – optional cheat sheet

content.whyBenefit: You can do any research in 30–45 min – enter a question or upload a PDF, get answers with sources.
content.toolsIntro: Enter a question or upload a PDF – you'll get answers with sources. Use with path step 71.3.

tools.name: Perplexity
tools.description: Search with sources. Instead of an hour of googling – enter a question, get an answer with links.

tools.name: PaperGuide
tools.description: Talk to your PDF. Upload a report or study – get summary, conclusions, main points.

tools.name: Scite
tools.description: Does the source really support your claim? Shows how the study is cited – supports or contradicts.

tools.name: Elicit
tools.description: Finds and summarises research by topic. Enter a question – get trends, risks, examples.

tools.name: Consensus
tools.description: Searches only in research papers and answers "what does science say?" – shows if studies support or contradict a claim.

tools.name: Connected Papers
tools.description: Shows how papers connect and which "research clusters" exist; helps find key (seminal) work.

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short (20 s)
sections.body: Optional cheat sheet: 6 AI tools for search and synthesis – use with path step **71.3** (parallel research). Typical run – 30–45 min.

sections.heading: Usage tips
sections.body:
**Perplexity:** web mode, ask for sources, be specific.
**PaperGuide:** upload PDF, ask a concrete question.
**Scite:** check "Supported" vs "Contradicted".
**Elicit:** clear question, trends and risks.
**Consensus / Connected Papers:** scientific claims and paper networks.

sections.heading: Typical run (30–45 min)
sections.body:
**4 tools = full run:**

**1.** Sources (Perplexity) – 5–10 min.

**2.** PDF analysis (PaperGuide / Elicit) – 10–15 min.

**3.** Citation check (Scite) – ~5 min.

**4.** Synthesis (Elicit) – 10–15 min.

slides.title: Weak spots – what to add
slides.subtitle: Optional: deeper practice – models, data, validation

sections.heading: 1️⃣ Why here?
sections.body: This optional slide shows where the basic AI analysis path ends and deeper practice begins. If you want to work more professionally, you need model logic, real data, automation and validation.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Models: forecast or classification?
sections.body: **Forecasting** helps answer what may happen (for example, next month sales). **Classification** helps assign a record to a group (for example, risky customer / not risky). You do not need mathematics here – the key is knowing which decision the model supports.
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a data analysis teacher.
TASK: Explain simply whether my task needs forecasting or classification.
CONTEXT: My task: [write]. Available data: [write].
OUTPUT: 1) Which model type fits 2) Why 3) Which column is needed as the target 4) What not to do at this stage.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Real data
sections.body: For practice, use public and reliable sources: Eurostat, open data portals, data catalogs and exports from your CRM or accounting tools. Before analysis, record the source, date and what each column means.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Automation
sections.body: Automation means repeated steps follow a clear rule: collect, clean, merge, create a report. Start not with code, but with process places where manual work repeats.
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a process analyst.
TASK: Find 3 places where my analysis process can be automated.
CONTEXT: My process: [describe steps]. What repeats: [write]. Tools I use: [write].
OUTPUT: 1) Automation point 2) Why it is worth it 3) Simplest first step 4) Risk.

sections.heading: 5️⃣ Validation
sections.body: Validation means checking whether the AI answer is based on data, not guessing. Link to hallucinations and knowledge-check slides: ask for sources, compare with the original table, check numbers and mark unclear places.
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a data quality checker.
TASK: Check the AI analysis answer using an anti-hallucination checklist.
INPUT: [paste AI answer and, if available, original data]
OUTPUT: 1) Where the answer is based on data 2) Where it may be guessing 3) Which numbers must be recalculated 4) What to fix before showing leadership.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want to understand more?
sections.body: These topics are the deeper level. If today you are only learning the path, remember the principle: **a model helps decide**, **data must be clear**, **automation starts with process**, and **validation protects against wrong conclusions**.

slides.title: Extra: visualization basics – psychology and grouping
slides.subtitle: Why visualization works (10/20/80) and 8 visual clarity principles

sections.heading: 1️⃣ In short
sections.body: Visualization helps you see relationships, risks or change faster – not just decorate a table.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ 10 / 20 / 80 principle
sections.body: **10%** – what people only hear. **20%** – what they read. **80%** – what they see and experience. If you want a decision, show data so it is understood quickly.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Eight visual grouping principles
sections.body:
**Proximity** – nearby elements look related.
**Similarity** – same color/shape = same meaning.
**Emphasis** – one clear focus.
**Connection** – lines show links.
**Continuity** – eye follows direction.
**Closure** – complete shapes.
**Figure and ground** – key element stands out.
**Common fate** – same direction = connection.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Do now
sections.body:
Check one dashboard or chart against these principles.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a visual clarity auditor.
TASK: Review my dashboard or chart description against 8 visual grouping principles.
INPUT: [paste dashboard description, screenshot text or chart list]
OUTPUT: 1) What is clear 2) Where confusion may arise 3) Which principle I break 4) 5 concrete fixes.

sections.heading: 🔽 Want to understand more?
sections.body: Chart type must match the question; design principles help the audience see the answer faster.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Quality check (1 min)

slides.title: Extra: story model and good dashboards
slides.subtitle: Sales path – executive dashboard and KPI hierarchy

sections.heading: 1️⃣ Why here?
sections.body: Leadership often does not need another table. It needs to understand what is changing, why it matters and what to do. That is why data must become a story.

rows: Clarity: what a person sees in 5 seconds

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Good dashboard
sections.body: In a good report, key metrics are on top, the trend is visible immediately, data is grouped, and filters help answer the next question. The first screen should not say “there is a lot of data”; it should say “this is the key insight”.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ BBC / Netflix principle
sections.body: **BBC style:** context, visual hierarchy, clear legend. **Netflix style:** quick comparison, segments, filters and recommendation. Both remind us: the visual should guide the viewer from question to decision.

sections.heading: 5️⃣ Do now
sections.body:
Ask AI not only to create a chart, but also to critique your dashboard logic.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a dashboard and data story critic.
TASK: Evaluate my dashboard structure.
INPUT: [paste dashboard description, metrics or screenshot text]
OUTPUT: 1) What the main story is 2) Whether top metrics are clear 3) Where context is missing 4) Where the audience may misunderstand 5) 5 fixes before showing leadership.

sections.heading: 🔽 Pro level
sections.body: When you want to go deeper, add analysis of bad visualizations, color psychology, interactivity principles and a method for choosing key metrics. In this module, the base is enough: clear story, clear hierarchy, clear action.

slides.title: Extra: AI assistance + super prompt + alternatives
slides.subtitle: 4 areas; Julius AI, Echobase, DataLab, Power BI

sections.heading: 1️⃣ Why here?
sections.body: AI can speed up visualization work, but it needs a clear task: what data, who will see it and which decision is needed. This slide combines ideas, code, guidance and alternative tools.

sections.heading: 2️⃣ Idea generation
sections.body: AI can suggest which chart to choose, which questions to ask and which story to look for. This is useful at the start, when you do not yet know what matters most in the data.

sections.heading: 3️⃣ Code generation
sections.body: AI can prepare examples for Python, SQL or Power BI formulas. Always check whether the code matches your column names and whether the result matches the original data.

sections.heading: 4️⃣ Visualization guidance
sections.body: AI can suggest rules for colors, grouping, metrics and legends. Use this as a checklist, not as the final design decision.

sections.heading: 5️⃣ Contextual analysis
sections.body: AI can explain what a chart means for the audience: leadership, team or customers. The same chart can tell a different story to different people.

sections.heading: 6️⃣ Super prompt
sections.body:
Run this prompt when you have a CSV or at least a clear data description and want one analysis package.

🔘 Copy prompt (below)
sections.copyable:
ROLE: You are a data analyst + visualization designer + business strategist.
TASK: Create a full analysis package from the provided data.
CONTEXT: CSV file or table with data: [describe columns, time period and audience].
OUTPUT:
1) Data summary (3 key insights)
2) Suggested charts
3) Power BI instruction
4) Python code direction or example
5) Story text for leadership (up to 200 words)
6) Recommended business action.

rows: When you want to analyze a table quickly without many technical steps
When a team needs agents and file synchronization
When you need SQL, Python and notebook-style work
When you need business reports, integrations and many visualizations

sections.heading: 🔽 Want to understand more?
sections.body: The full visualization and data story path is planned for later modules. The main takeaway here: AI speeds up the work, but you still control the question, audience and decision.

questions.question: How many steps does the data analysis flow (pipeline) have?
options: 6 steps: collect, prepare, EDA, models, visualization, publish
8 steps – the same as the full analysis template
questions.explanation: The analysis flow has 6 steps (collect → prepare → EDA → models → visualization → publish). The full analysis template is a separate 8-step model.

questions.question: How many steps does the full analysis template have (one topic)?
options: 8 steps: sources, structure, cleaning, EDA, visuals, insights, forecasts, recommendations
6 steps – the same as the analysis flow
5 steps: META, INPUT, OUTPUT, REASONING, QUALITY
questions.explanation: The full analysis template has 8 steps: sources, structure, cleaning, EDA, visuals, insights, forecasts, recommendations.

questions.question: A manager asks “Why did Q3 sales drop?” and “What should we do next?” – which two analysis types match?
questions.explanation: “Why?” matches diagnostic analysis; “What to do?” matches prescriptive. See the “4 analysis types” slide.

questions.question: Module 9 has two practices. When pick the sources catalog vs your CSV/Excel?
options: Sources catalog – when you lack internal data; CSV/Excel – when you have a CRM/Excel export or the sample file
Always only the sources catalog – CSV is optional forever
Always only your file – public sources are out of scope
Both practices only after the hub scenarios
questions.explanation: Without internal data, start with a public sources catalog. If you have Excel/CRM (or the course sample CSV) – cleaning practice with a file in AI chat.

slides.subtitle: Data path, leadership report, full analysis template

testQuestions.question: A colleague sent a CSV without clear column types (dates as text, empty rows). What should you do first before analysis?
options: Run the full analysis template immediately
Clean first: types, dates, duplicates, missing values – then analyze
Draw a chart for leadership immediately
Skip the file and use only public sources
testQuestions.explanation: Without cleaning, AI and charts mislead. First – types, dates, duplicates, missing values; then the analysis flow or full template.
testQuestions.scenarioContext: A colleague sent a CSV export from a legacy system – column types unclear, dates mixed.

testQuestions.question: Which BI schema step is responsible for "what to show leadership"?
testQuestions.explanation: Report includes visualization and conclusions – how to show leadership.

testQuestions.question: How many steps does MASTER PROMPT have for full data analysis?
testQuestions.explanation: MASTER PROMPT has 8 steps: sources, structure, cleaning, EDA, visualizations, insights, forecasts, recommendations.

testQuestions.scenarioContext: You have 200 customer reviews about a product. You want not only "positive/neutral/negative" but also most common topics, emotion intensity and priority action recommendations.
testQuestions.question: What to include in a sentiment analysis prompt?
options: OUTPUT with 5 points: sentiment structure, topics (with frequency), intensity, critical complaints, 3 priority actions
testQuestions.explanation: Business value comes from topics, intensity, priorities and action recommendations; OUTPUT must cover a structured result with these points.

testQuestions.scenarioContext: Leadership asks: "Why did Q3 sales drop?" and "How should we act next?"
testQuestions.question: Which two analysis types match these two questions?
options: First – diagnostic (Why?), second – prescriptive (How to act?)
testQuestions.explanation: 4 analysis types: descriptive (What happened?), diagnostic (Why?), predictive (What may happen?), prescriptive (How to act?).

testQuestions.scenarioContext: Leadership needs sales trends over 12 months and segment profitability presented. You have cleaned data.
testQuestions.question: How to formulate a visualization prompt to get useful result for leadership?
options: Specify data, goal (trends, segments, profitability), audience (leadership) and ask for insights below the chart
Ask for all possible chart types at once
Ask for only tables without visualizations
testQuestions.explanation: A good visualization prompt specifies data, goal, audience and asks for insights – not just a "pretty chart".

testQuestions.scenarioContext: You exported CRM contacts and deals to Excel. Dates are mixed (DD/MM and MM/DD), duplicates exist. Before descriptive and diagnostic analysis you must prepare the data.
testQuestions.question: What belongs in the data-cleaning checklist before analysis?
options: Anonymization, format alignment, duplicate removal, missing-value identification, data-type validation, time/region tags
Only the full analysis template with no cleaning
testQuestions.explanation: The cleaning checklist covers anonymization, formats, duplicates, missing values, validation and tags – before any analysis. Same logic as Data preparation.

testQuestions.question: What is the correct prompt sequence (workflow) order for business analysis?
options: Collect → Clean → Metadata → Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive → Action plan
testQuestions.explanation: Correct sequence: collect data, clean (checklist), add metadata, then 4 analysis types and action plan.

testQuestions.scenarioContext: CFO asks for help: need to assess EBITDA, margin, cash flow and debt level, and identify 3 financial risks and 3 optimization opportunities.
testQuestions.question: Which prompt type best matches this business need?
options: Role activation only: "You are a business analyst"
Role (CFO assistant) + concrete OUTPUT points: EBITDA, margin, cash flow, debt, 3 risks, 3 optimization opportunities
testQuestions.explanation: Business analysis needs a clear role (CFO assistant) and structured OUTPUT – what exactly AI must output (metrics, risks, opportunities).

branching.question: Which analysis would you run first in this situation?

choices.label: Descriptive (What happened?)
choices.consequence: Correct – first you need to summarize the data. "What happened?" gives the big picture; then it is easier to ask diagnostic and predictive questions.

choices.label: Diagnostic (Why?)
choices.consequence: You can start from cause – if you already know something happened (e.g. Q3 drop). Diagnostics then helps find causes; later add descriptive and predictive.

choices.label: Predictive (What may happen?)
choices.consequence: Forecast without descriptive/diagnostic analysis can be unfounded. Recommended: first "What happened?" and "Why?", then "What may happen?" – insights will be stronger.

content.reflectionPromptAfter:
META: You are a training reflection assistant. Goal – consolidate the Data scraping scenario experience.
INPUT: I just completed the Data scraping scenario – data collection with an AI-generated Python script.
OUTPUT: Ask me one question: what did this scenario give me and what will I apply next time? After my answer give 1 concrete tip.

scenario.context: Data Analysis path data collection step. AI generates a Python script (requests, BeautifulSoup); you run it – pip install, python scrape.py.
scenario.data: Website link and what to extract (table, list, prices).
scenario.constraints: Public data only. Libraries: requests, BeautifulSoup, or pandas.read_html.
scenario.expectedFormat: CSV file with UTF-8 encoding. Optional: simple chart from collected data.

practicalTask.template:
ROLE: You are a Python developer who writes simple data scraping scripts.
TASK: Write a Python script that:
1. Opens this page: [KEEP_WEBSITE_URL]
2. Extracts [WHAT_TO_EXTRACT – e.g. a table with prices, product list, names and prices]
3. Saves the result to a CSV file (UTF-8 encoding)

Requirements:
- Use requests and BeautifulSoup (or pandas.read_html if it is a table).
- Script should be short, with comments in English.
- At the end print: how many records collected and the file name.
Provide the full script – so I can copy to a file and run: pip install requests beautifulsoup4, python scrape.py
practicalTask.templateLabel: Prompt to generate script – copy to AI
practicalTask.title: Get Python script and collect data
practicalTask.placeholder: Enter the website URL, what you collected, or the AI answer.

steps.title: Copy the prompt, enter the URL and what to collect – get a script from AI
steps.description: Replace [KEEP_WEBSITE_URL] and [WHAT_TO_EXTRACT]. AI will provide a script – copy it to scrape.py.

steps.title: Run the script
steps.description: In the terminal: pip install requests beautifulsoup4 (one time). Then: python scrape.py.
steps.hint: Result – CSV in the same folder.

slides.title: Project summary
slides.subtitle: What next?

content.introBody: Congratulations! You completed the core path: 8-step cycle, summary or dashboard.

sections.heading: Example result (workflow)
sections.body: A good result: a consolidated document with sources, deduplicated entries, clear KPIs or charts, .html or a clear spec for leadership. If you used the MASTER PROMPT from Module 7, the structure should be similar: sources, structure, cleaning, insights, recommendations.

sections.heading: What next?
sections.body: Within 48 hours, repeat the workflow for another topic or open an extra scenario. Return to the module list — next module or certificate.

sections.heading: Reflection
sections.body: What did you do? (What) — What does it mean for you or the team? (So What) — What will you do next? (Now What). Answer briefly to yourself or use the prompt below in AI.

content.reflectionPrompt:
META: You are a training reflection assistant. Goal – consolidate Data Analysis path project results.
INPUT: I just finished Module 9 project – business analysis with AI (8-step workflow and at least one scenario).
OUTPUT: Ask 3 questions: (1) What did I do in this project and where will I apply it? (2) What does this mean for me or the team? (3) What will I do in the next 24–48 hours? After my answers give 1 concrete tip.
content.tagline: One workflow cycle = one ready project.
